content.text:
Introducing Pastery.

A clipboard app for macOS that brings up all your previous copied items with a single hotkey.

No more switching between windows. Copy everything at once, paste seamlessly where you need it. All file formats, customisable hotkeys, feels native rather than installed.

hot take: Figma has made designers worse.

we prototype pixel-perfect screens nobody asked for instead of solving the actual problem on paper first.

ride._note: Designed to trip the controversy guard: replies (181) >> likes (41). High views via argument, not value.

content.text:
Crazy times in design industry. Wanted to rant a bit about something I've been noticing.

People with barely any real experience with AI are launching AI & design courses. You learnt what a PR and a merge is only a few weeks ago, calm down.

Saw someone selling a PDF for 'how to crack interviews at top design companies'... for companies they couldn't crack themselves.

Feels like when things get boring, people pivot to building an audience, and when that doesn't stick, they start selling courses.

Everyone's chasing attention. Very few are building actual depth. And the cost? Early designers already anxious about AI end up paying for noise.

If you're an experienced designer who mentors aspiring designers, paid or free, I salute you.

For those early and liking the attention: please be responsible. There's a lot to learn.

Second year of building my dream design agency.

2025 gave us 23 clients, 38 projects, and a team of 9.

Here's a small glimpse of our work at bricxlabs.com

I audited 50 B2B SaaS onboardings. 41 made the same mistake:

they ask the user to configure before they let them feel the value.

Configuration is work. Value is the reward. You're charging rent before showing the apartment. 🧵

ride._note: This author's own breakout -> should surface as PEAK (high baseline AND high breakout).

content.text:
We redesigned one SaaS checkout flow.

Conversion went 3.1% -> 4.6% in three weeks. No new features. Just UX.

Here's the exact teardown 🧵

1/ The old flow asked for billing before the user saw value...
2/ We moved the 'aha' moment ahead of the form...
3/ One field per screen beat the single long form by 31%...

engine._note: Stand-in for a real Tier-2 build-in-public author (e.g. @thepatwalls / @levelsio) until that data is collected. Designed as the DURABLE engine exemplar: consistently high bookmarks across posts.

content.text:
How we cut onboarding from 6 steps to 2 and activation jumped 23%.

The trick wasn't removing steps. It was removing decisions. 🧵

Pricing page teardown: a $40M ARR SaaS vs a $0 one.

The difference isn't the price. It's that one anchors on outcomes and the other lists features. Breakdown 🧵

Empty states are the most underpriced real estate in SaaS.

Most teams ship 'No data yet.' The good ones ship a one-click path to the first win. Here's 5 done right 🧵

Your settings page is a graveyard of indecision.

Every toggle you add is a decision you couldn't make for the user. Defaults are a design skill. 🧵
which hero section are you picking?

who's the best motion designer making SaaS launch videos right now?

asking for a project. tag them.

who has the best seo agency?

we need someone who understands search intent, ranking on AI search, and has AI processes dialed in to rank on search.

hiring: X content strategist

you need to be a designer who literally lives on X.

you will research trends and create viral content around it.

full time role. dm me with your top 3 posts.

a good design partner doesn't ask 'what do you want it to look like.'

they ask 'what number are we trying to move.'

that's the whole difference.

Second year of building my dream design agency.

2025 gave us 23 clients, 38 projects, and a team of 9.

Here's a small glimpse of our work at bricxlabs.com

People with barely any real experience with AI are launching AI & design courses. You learnt what a PR and a merge is only a few weeks ago, calm down.

spent the morning redoing the empty states for CREW.

an empty state is not a dead end, it's the best onboarding surface you have. show the user what 'full' looks like.
reminder: a doodle a day keeps the creative block away. here's today's.

things I wish someone told me in year 1 of product design:

- the spec is a hypothesis, not a contract
- ship the boring fix before the clever one
- your craft means nothing if the eng team can't build it

content.text: small interaction, big delight: when a toggle animates the consequence, not just itself. users trust what they can see happen.

Introducing Pastery.

A clipboard app for macOS that brings up all your previous copied items with a single hotkey.

No more switching between windows. Copy everything at once, paste seamlessly where you need it. All file formats, customisable hotkeys, feels native rather than installed.
shipped a tiny update to Pastery: pinned items. the things you paste 50x a day now sit at the top. small, but it's the feature I use most.
building in public lesson: people don't bookmark features, they bookmark outcomes. 'paste anything in one hotkey' > 'new clipboard manager'.
generalist designer truth: the more you can build, the less you have to explain. a working prototype ends 90% of design debates.

2/ We moved the 'aha' moment ahead of the form...
3/ One field per screen beat the single long form by 31%...
